Convert your daily step count to calories burned.

howItWorks.intro: Two numbers drive the answer: how far your steps carried you, and how much body weight you moved that distance. A step is roughly 0.04 calories, but that hides a wide range — a 130 lb person and a 220 lb person can differ by 200+ calories over the same 10,000 steps.
howItWorks.outro: This estimates ordinary flat-ground walking. Hills, stairs, a loaded backpack, or a brisk pace all push the real burn higher than the number shown.

faq.question: How many calories does one step burn?
faq.answer: Roughly 0.04 calories for an average adult, so about 40 calories per 1,000 steps. Your exact figure scales with weight: a 200 lb walker burns close to 0.05 per step, while a 120 lb walker is nearer 0.03.

faq.question: Does my weight change how many calories I burn walking?
faq.answer: Yes, more than almost any other factor. Calories burned rises in near-direct proportion to body weight, because you are carrying that weight every step. A 180 lb person burns about 40% more over the same distance than a 130 lb person.

faq.question: Is 10,000 steps a day enough to lose weight?
faq.answer: 10,000 steps burns roughly 300–500 calories depending on your weight — meaningful, but weight loss depends on total intake versus output. Paired with a modest calorie deficit it can drive steady loss; on its own it mostly offsets a snack or two.

faq.question: Does walking burn fewer calories than running the same distance?
faq.answer: Over the same distance the totals are surprisingly close, since both move your body weight the same number of miles. Running burns a bit more per mile from the airborne push-off and covers ground far faster, so running wins on calories per minute, not per mile.

faq.question: How far is 10,000 steps in miles?
faq.answer: About 4.5 to 5 miles for an average stride of roughly 2.2 to 2.5 feet. Taller people with longer strides hit 5 miles sooner; a shorter stride can stretch 10,000 steps closer to 4 miles.

faq.question: Do steps from housework and errands count?
faq.answer: Yes. Every step moves your body weight the same way, so pacing on a phone call, cleaning, or walking the aisles all burn calories and add to your daily total, even if a tracker labels them as untracked activity.

howWeCalculate.intro: The calculator first turns your steps into distance, then multiplies that distance by your body weight to get calories. Distance is what makes weight matter — you burn calories per mile carried, not per step tapped.
steps: Convert steps to miles: about 2,000 average steps equal one mile, so 10,000 steps ≈ 5 miles.
Apply the walking burn: calories ≈ distance(miles) × weight(lbs) × 0.53, the standard MET-based rate for a moderate walking pace.
Worked example: a 180 lb person walking 10,000 steps (~4.5 miles) burns roughly 430 calories.
Everything runs in your browser as you type — change your weight or step count and the calories update instantly.
howWeCalculate.disclaimer: These are estimates for level-ground walking at a moderate pace. Actual burn shifts with terrain, incline, pace, stride length, and individual metabolism — treat the result as a ballpark, not a lab measurement.

whenToUse.intro: Reach for this when a step count is the number you have and calories is the number you want — from a fitness tracker, a treadmill readout, or a rough guess at your daily walking.
scenarios: Tracking a calorie deficit for weight loss and needing to credit the calories your daily walks actually burn
Checking whether hitting a 10,000-step goal covers the dessert you're eyeing before you commit to it
Comparing a slow 8,000-step day against a brisk 12,000-step day to see how much the extra steps add
Estimating burn for a heavier or lighter walking partner, since the same route burns very different amounts by weight
Setting a realistic step target when you want to walk off a specific number of calories per day

sections.title: What Is the Steps to Calories Calculator?
sections.body: This calculator turns a step count into calories burned, using your body weight to make the estimate personal instead of generic. Enter your steps and weight, and it returns calories burned along with the distance you covered and roughly how long that walk took. The engine behind it is simple: about 2,000 steps make a mile, and each mile burns calories in proportion to how much you weigh. That's why a 130 lb walker and a 200 lb walker can log the same 10,000 steps and end up hundreds of calories apart.

sections.title: How Weight and Pace Change the Number
sections.body: Weight is the biggest lever here. Calories burned rises almost in step with body weight, so a 220 lb person walking 10,000 steps burns close to 500 calories while a 120 lb person on the same route is nearer 270. Pace matters too, but less than most expect: over a fixed distance, walking fast and walking slow burn similar totals because you move the same body weight the same number of miles. Where pace pays off is per minute — a brisk 3.5 mph walk finishes your steps sooner and packs the burn into less time.

sections.title: Reading the Result Honestly
sections.body: Take a concrete case: a 180 lb person walking 10,000 steps (~4.5 miles) burns roughly 430 calories on flat ground at an easy pace. Add hills, stairs, a stroller, or a loaded pack and the real figure climbs well above that. The number here assumes ordinary level walking, so use it as a planning estimate for a step goal or calorie deficit — not as an exact figure to eat back calorie-for-calorie.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Steps
howToSteps.description: Type in your step count — pull it straight from your phone, watch, or fitness tracker for the most accurate distance and calorie estimate.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Weight (lbs)
howToSteps.description: Enter your body weight in pounds. This is the single biggest factor in the result, since heavier walkers burn more calories over the same distance.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit Calculate to convert your steps into miles and then into calories burned. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your calories burned, the distance you covered, and the estimated walking time. Adjust the step count or weight to compare a slower day against a longer one.

tips: Use the step count from your tracker rather than a guess — being off by 2,000 steps shifts the calorie estimate by 80 to 100 calories.
If you want to burn more without adding steps, pick a hillier route or add stairs; incline raises the burn that flat-ground numbers assume away.
Don't eat back the full calorie figure when dieting — treat it as an upper bound, since trackers and formulas both tend to run generous.
Compare two weights side by side to see why a lighter walking partner has to log more steps to match your calorie burn.
To hit a specific calorie target, divide it by your per-1,000-step burn (roughly weight × 0.02) to find the step goal you need.
